skill.name: skill-creation-guide
skill.description: Guides AI agents through planning, writing, validating, and packaging reusable skills with clear instructions and bundled resources.
skill.summary: Create focused AI skills with practical structure, reusable resources, validation checks, and packaging guidance.
skill.author: notedit

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many issues, but most are false positives from Markdown backticks, Apache license text, and ordinary documentation terms. The real residual risk is local filesystem behavior in helper scripts that create files and recursively package directories. No evidence found of malicious network activity, credential access, obfuscation, prompt injection, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Filesystem Writes in Initializer
medium_findings.description: The initializer builds its target directory from command-line input, creates directories, writes several files, and sets executable permissions. This is intended behavior for a skill generator, but it does not enforce the printed skill-name constraints before writing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file creation and chmod operations use command-line values. The script documents naming rules but does not validate skill_name before constructing the output path.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Packaging May Include Unintended Files
medium_findings.description: The package script recursively archives every file under the selected skill path. This is legitimate packaging behavior, but a wrong input path or secret file inside the tree could disclose unintended local files in the generated package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive archive logic is visible and broad. The concern depends on operator input and local file contents, so it is a moderate operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Benign License URLs Flagged as Network Activity
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are standard Apache License references. They are plain text in a license file and are not used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are inside Apache License text. No code path reads these URLs for network access.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misread as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings point to Markdown fences, inline filenames, and examples. The reviewed Python scripts do not invoke shell commands or subprocess APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is documentation formatting and template text. No shell execution primitive appears in the reviewed executable files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings occur in license prose, Markdown examples, validation text, and ordinary skill documentation. No cryptographic API or hash algorithm implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain prose or validation strings, not crypto calls. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms being used for security.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Local Validation Checks
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance flags map to checks for file existence, YAML frontmatter, and skill structure. These checks support local validation and do not inventory the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operations are limited to paths supplied for skill validation. No evidence found of host enumeration, process listing, or credential discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Path Construction Before Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The initializer combines user-provided arguments into a resolved output path and then writes generated files. Validate skill names and output paths before running it on untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path and skill name come from argv and are used before validation. The script is local tooling, so the risk is bounded by who runs it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Archive Creation Without Exclusions
dangerous_patterns.description: The packager writes all files under the chosen directory into a skill archive. Review the directory before packaging to avoid including private or unrelated files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package loop uses rglob over the full input tree. The impact depends on local directory contents and user-selected path.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent guidance when turning repeatable AI work into reusable skills. This skill provides structure, templates, validation steps, and packaging guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to structure SKILL.md metadata, body instructions, and bundled resources.
Guides users through planning concrete examples before writing a new skill.
Provides an initializer script that creates a template skill directory with sample resources.
Provides a validator for required frontmatter, naming rules, and description checks.
Provides a packager that validates a skill and creates a distributable .skill archive.
Links to reference guidance for workflow patterns and consistent output formats.
limitations: It does not enforce all marketplace quality rules automatically.
Generated template files require user review and customization before publishing.
The helper scripts operate on local paths and should be run only on intended directories.
It does not provide domain-specific content for the skill being created.

use_cases.title: Create a New Internal Skill
use_cases.description: Plan examples, scaffold the directory, write focused instructions, and package the result for internal agent use.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Skill
use_cases.description: Review structure, move long details into references, add reusable scripts, and validate the updated skill.

use_cases.title: Prepare Marketplace Submissions
use_cases.description: Check metadata, remove unnecessary files, validate frontmatter, and produce a distributable skill archive.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Beginner Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the goal is clear but the skill structure is not defined.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a new skill for [task]. Ask for the minimum examples needed, then propose the SKILL.md structure and resource files.

prompt_templates.title: Scaffold a Skill Directory
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when ready to create the first editable version.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new skill named [skill-name] in [output directory]. Use the initializer, then explain which generated files I should customize or delete.

prompt_templates.title: Refine an Existing Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a draft skill is too long, unclear, or hard to trigger.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill draft. Make the trigger description specific, reduce unnecessary context, and move detailed material into references when useful.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Package for Release
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the skill is ready for final checks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate and package this skill for distribution. Report validation failures, fix scoped issues, and confirm the final archive contents before release.

output_examples.input: A user wants a skill for repeatable PDF editing tasks.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies common workflows, recommends a task-based structure, proposes a small helper script, and lists required validation checks.

output_examples.input: A draft skill has a long SKILL.md file and several detailed examples.
output_examples.output: The assistant keeps core workflow instructions in SKILL.md and moves detailed examples into reference files with clear loading guidance.

output_examples.input: A maintainer needs to release a finished skill.
output_examples.output: The assistant validates metadata and frontmatter, reviews local files for unnecessary content, and prepares a distributable skill archive.

best_practices: Start with concrete user examples before choosing scripts, references, or assets.
Keep SKILL.md concise and move detailed optional guidance into linked reference files.
Run validation and inspect package contents before sharing a skill archive.
anti_patterns: Do not add broad documentation files that do not support the skill workflow.
Do not hide important trigger conditions only inside the skill body.
Do not package directories before checking for private or unrelated files.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps create reusable AI skills with clear triggers, concise instructions, bundled resources, validation, and packaging steps.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is useful for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code skill workflows.

faq.question: Does it write the full domain content for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure and process, but the domain-specific knowledge must come from the user or project materials.

faq.question: When should I add scripts to a skill?
faq.answer: Add scripts when a task needs repeatable, deterministic behavior that is safer than rewriting instructions each time.

faq.question: Why use references instead of one large SKILL.md?
faq.answer: References keep the main skill concise while making detailed guidance available only when needed.

faq.question: What should I check before packaging?
faq.answer: Validate frontmatter, confirm the directory is correct, remove unnecessary files, and inspect for private information.
